title: 329 million children in India exposed to extreme heat: UNICEF report
description: A UN report reveals 92% of Indian children face extreme heat, with 89 million exposed to heatwaves. While India has Heat Action Plans, they lack child-specific measures. India's high hazard exposure score, especially for extreme heat and air pollution, highlights the urgent need for targeted interventions to protect young lives.

The Animal actor shared a striking selfie of the father-son duo twinning in black, captioned: "Hey my Aryaman, my angel...Happy Birthday, Love you most." The post garnered affectionate wishes from industry peers, including actor Rahul Dev and businessman Raj Kundra. Born on June 16, 2001, to Bobby and Tanya Deol, Aryaman typically stays out of the limelight, having made his first major public appearance at the IIFA Awards in 2018.

Political parties are urging vigilance to prevent the deletion of genuine voters.

Dell CEO Michael Dell makes one of largest public university donations in US history
description: Michael Dell has gifted a staggering $750 million to the University of Texas at Austin, propelling the creation of a groundbreaking AI-native hospital and research campus. This substantial donation, adding to his family's legacy of support, will also bolster scholarships, advanced computing, and vital medical research, underscoring Dell's enduring connection to his alma mater.

title: US senator says Tesla benefited from govt support, Elon Musk replies
description: Elon Musk has refuted claims that his companies, Tesla and SpaceX, owe their success to government aid, stating such incentives represent less than 2% of their value. The debate ignited between Senators Mike Lee and Ed Markey over taxpayer contributions to Musk's wealth. Musk asserted that removing EV tax credits actually boosted Tesla's market share.

title: Microsoft president Brad Smith has a message for students booing tech CEOs
description: Microsoft president Brad Smith has responded to graduating students who booed AI at commencement ceremonies this spring, calling the backlash a "powerful wake-up call for the tech sector." In a 3,000-word essay, Smith acknowledged the class of 2026 faces a "perfect storm" of AI automation and tech layoffs, yet urged graduates to adapt rather than fear AI. His optimistic message arrives as Sam Altman, Mustafa Suleyman, and Dario Amodei clash over AI's job impact.
